CONFIGURED-panel review (one of 2; return verdict JSON, write NOTHING). Round 1.
Task: T74 (goals:G5) — "Wire deferred boot-time resolution and the resolution loop into the device lifecycle" (full text: fetch_item tasks T74 — includes the R70 first-resolve-install-path requirements)
Worktree: /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-afa803909d4c484b6 (branch implement/T74, base 352c1e5)
Go repo — gate incl. `go test ./internal/device/... -race` via nix develop.
acceptance: go test ./internal/device/... -race passes: (1) Up succeeds with a never-resolving fake resolver on a single-hostname peer (tolerant boot; UAPI get shows NO peer endpoint); (2) first successful resolve INSTALLS the engine peer endpoint AND a handshake initiation actually egresses toward the resolved address (initiation packet observed at the test bind; rehandshake-counter alone insufficient, R70); (3) Close stops the loop with no goroutine leak under -race; (4) zero-hostname configs construct no resolver and start no loop. Full go test ./... and go vet ./... pass.
Worker: pass; commit a66df75; files internal/device/{device.go, failover.go, failover_test.go, resolution_test.go, lifecycle_test.go}, docs/design.md, docs/install.md. Design: deviceInstallEndpoint via UAPI endpoint= then rehandshake; separate Tunnel.stopResolution in Close; resolver built only when a hostname spec exists; injectable up() seam. Scrutinize: (a) the endpoint-less tolerant boot really brings the tunnel up with no peer endpoint (UAPI get shows none) and doesn't hard-fail; (b) Multipath.ParseEndpoint stays IP-only (no hostname reaches the bind); (c) the install path is genuinely observed by an egressing initiation packet, not just a rehandshake counter; (d) the stopResolution ordering in Close (between stopHubFailover and dev.Close) and leak-freedom; (e) whether leaving Reload untouched is a legitimate scope call or a gap.
Return { taskId, verdict, criticism: [], questions: [], defects: [], rationale, summary }.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.

Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.

- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").

TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

diff --git a/docs/design.md b/docs/design.md
index bf48a7f..74ed68b 100644
--- a/docs/design.md
+++ b/docs/design.md
@@ -327,8 +327,27 @@ its goroutine; results are applied only through `updateResolution`. Each evaluat
   strand the bond on).
 
 This controller runs **even for a single-hostname peer** (to track a changing DDNS
-address), independent of hub-failover's `>= 2` guard; the first successful poll of a
-hostname-only peer is what boot-adopts its active endpoint.
+address), independent of hub-failover's `>= 2` guard; the first successful resolution
+of a hostname-only peer is what boot-adopts its active endpoint.
+
+**Device lifecycle** (T74). At `Up` the device does one **bounded initial resolve**
+of each hostname spec (the `[dns]` per-lookup timeout) and builds the engine/UAPI peer
+endpoint **only from resolved entries** — the flattened head of the seeded specs. If a
+name does not resolve in the boot window (single-hostname peer, resolver down), the
+tunnel comes up **without a peer endpoint** (tolerant boot, Q30 defer-and-reconcile —
+an unresolvable name never hard-fails bring-up); the concentrator already runs
+endpoint-less, so the engine supports it. The resolver is constructed **once**, and
+**only when some peer carries a hostname spec** — a zero-hostname config builds no
+resolver and starts no loop (Q29 inertness). The **first-resolve install path (R70)**
+is load-bearing: `SetPeerRemote` repoints the bind's per-path remotes but **never sets
+the engine peer's endpoint**, which is populated **only** by a UAPI `endpoint=` line
+routed through `Multipath.ParseEndpoint`. So after an endpoint-less boot, the first
+successful resolve must **install** the resolved endpoint on the engine peer via the
+UAPI/IpcSet path (`deviceInstallEndpoint`) **then** re-handshake — the initiation now
+has an addressable endpoint. Subsequent re-resolves of an already-installed peer take
+the normal `SetPeerRemote` repoint path (the engine's virtual endpoint stays stable per
+A1; only the bind remotes move). The re-resolution loop's stopper is held on the
+`Tunnel` and invoked by `Close` between the hub-failover stop and the engine teardown.
 
 ### Per-path telemetry — `internal/telemetry`

 - `doh_url`/`dot_server` are mode-specific: setting the wrong one for the
   selected `resolver` (or either one under `resolver = "system"`) is
   rejected at load.
+- **Tolerant boot**: a hostname endpoint that cannot be resolved at startup
+  (resolver down, DNS outage) never fails bring-up — the tunnel comes up
+  *without* that peer endpoint and the background re-resolution loop installs
+  it and initiates the handshake on the first successful lookup. Steady-state
+  re-resolution then repoints the bond whenever the record changes.
 
 ### 3a. Tuning per-link bandwidth and pacing

+endpoint **only from resolved entries** — the flattened head of the seeded specs. If a
+name does not resolve in the boot window (single-hostname peer, resolver down), the

+an unresolvable name never hard-fails bring-up); the concentrator already runs
+endpoint-less, so the engine supports it. The resolver is constructed **once**, and
+**only when some peer carries a hostname spec** — a zero-hostname config builds no
+resolver and starts no loop (Q29 inertness). The **first-resolve install path (R70)**
+is load-bearing: `SetPeerRemote` repoints the bind's per-path remotes but **never sets
+the engine peer's endpoint**, which is populated **only** by a UAPI `endpoint=` line
+routed through `Multipath.ParseEndpoint`. So after an endpoint-less boot, the first
+successful resolve must **install** the resolved endpoint on the engine peer via the
+UAPI/IpcSet path (`deviceInstallEndpoint`) **then** re-handshake — the initiation now
+has an addressable endpoint. Subsequent re-resolves of an already-installed peer take
+the normal `SetPeerRemote` repoint path (the engine's virtual endpoint stays stable per
+A1; only the bind remotes move). The re-resolution loop's stopper is held on the
+`Tunnel` and invoked by `Close` between the hub-failover stop and the engine teardown.

1	package device
2	
3	import (
4		"context"

18		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
19		"github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/tun/tuntest"
20		"go.uber.org/goleak"
21	
22		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/bind"
23		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
24		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/dnsresolve"
25		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/sched"
26		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
27	)
28	
29	// genX25519 returns a fresh X25519 private/public key pair as raw 32-byte slices — valid curve
30	// material so the engine's CreateMessageInitiation (a DH against the peer static key) succeeds.
31	func genX25519(t *testing.T) (privRaw, pubRaw []byte) {
32		t.Helper()
33		k, err := ecdh.X25519().GenerateKey(rand.Reader)
34		if err != nil {
35			t.Fatalf("generate X25519 key: %v", err)
36		}
37		return k.Bytes(), k.PublicKey().Bytes()
38	}
39	
40	// keyFromRaw builds a config.Key from raw key bytes (via the only public constructor,
41	// UnmarshalText over base64), so a test can pass the SAME key to the engine (as hex UAPI) and to
42	// deviceRehandshake/deviceInstallEndpoint (as a config.Key).
43	func keyFromRaw(t *testing.T, raw []byte) config.Key {
44		t.Helper()
45		var k config.Key
46		if err := k.UnmarshalText([]byte(base64.StdEncoding.EncodeToString(raw))); err != nil {
47			t.Fatalf("build config.Key: %v", err)
48		}
49		return k
50	}
51	
52	// randB64Key returns 32 random bytes base64-encoded (a placeholder PSK / key for a config file).
53	func randB64Key(t *testing.T) string {
54		t.Helper()
55		var b [32]byte
56		if _, err := rand.Read(b[:]); err != nil {
57			t.Fatalf("read random: %v", err)
58		}
59		return base64.StdEncoding.EncodeToString(b[:])
60	}
61	
62	// writeEdgeConfig writes a minimal single-path edge config to a 0600 temp file and loads it. The
63	// endpoints TOML array literal (e.g. `["hub.example.com:51820"]`) and the dns opt-in are the only
64	// per-test variation; every other field is a fixed loopback default.
65	func writeEdgeConfig(t *testing.T, endpointsTOML string, dns bool) *config.Config {
66		t.Helper()
67		privRaw, _ := genX25519(t)
68		_, pubRaw := genX25519(t)
69		dnsLine := ""
70		if dns {
71			dnsLine = "dns = true\n"
72		}
73		body := fmt.Sprintf(`role = "edge"
74	psk = "%s"
75	
76	[[paths]]
77	name = "a"
78	source_addr = "127.0.0.1"
79	
80	[wireguard]
81	private_key = "%s"
82	
83	[[wireguard.peers]]
84	public_key = "%s"
85	%sendpoints = %s
86	allowed_ips = ["10.0.0.0/24"]
87	`, randB64Key(t),
88			base64.StdEncoding.EncodeToString(privRaw),
89			base64.StdEncoding.EncodeToString(pubRaw),
90			dnsLine, endpointsTOML)
91	
92		path := filepath.Join(t.TempDir(), "edge.toml")
93		if err := os.WriteFile(path, []byte(body), 0o600); err != nil {
94			t.Fatalf("write config: %v", err)
95		}
96		if err := os.Chmod(path, 0o600); err != nil { // defeat umask widening (config.Load requires 0600 exactly)
97			t.Fatalf("chmod config: %v", err)
98		}
99		cfg, err := config.Load(path)
100		if err != nil {
101			t.Fatalf("load config: %v", err)
102		}
103		return cfg
104	}
105	
106	// neverResolver is a resolver whose every lookup returns an error — a name that never resolves.
107	type neverResolver struct{ calls int }
108	
109	func (r *neverResolver) Lookup(_ context.Context, host string) ([]netip.Addr, time.Duration, bool, error) {
110		r.calls++
111		return nil, 0, false, fmt.Errorf("neverResolver: %q does not resolve", host)
112	}
113	
114	// TestUpTolerantBootEndpointless is acceptance (1): Up on a single-hostname peer whose name NEVER
115	// resolves must SUCCEED (tolerant boot, Q30 defer-and-reconcile — never hard-fail on an
116	// unresolvable name), and the engine peer must carry NO endpoint (UAPI get shows no endpoint=
117	// line) — the endpoint-less boot the re-resolution loop later completes on first success.
118	func TestUpTolerantBootEndpointless(t *testing.T) {
119		cfg := writeEdgeConfig(t, `["hub.example.com:51820"]`, true)
120		chtun := tuntest.NewChannelTUN()
121	
122		rslv := &neverResolver{}
123		factoryCalls := 0
124		factory := func() (dnsresolve.Resolver, error) {
125			factoryCalls++
126			return rslv, nil
127		}
128	
129		tun, err := up(cfg, discardLogger(t), chtun.TUN(), "wanbondtest0", factory)
130		if err != nil {
131			t.Fatalf("up on a never-resolving single-hostname peer failed, want tolerant boot: %v", err)
132		}
133		defer tun.Close()
134	
135		if factoryCalls != 1 {
136			t.Fatalf("resolver factory called %d times for a hostname peer, want exactly 1", factoryCalls)
137		}
138		if rslv.calls == 0 {
139			t.Fatalf("bounded initial resolve never attempted a lookup, want >= 1")
140		}
141	
142		uapi, err := tun.dev.IpcGet()
143		if err != nil {
144			t.Fatalf("IpcGet: %v", err)
145		}
146		if strings.Contains(uapi, "endpoint=") {
147			t.Fatalf("tolerant boot on an unresolvable hostname installed an endpoint, want NONE (endpoint-less boot):\n%s", uapi)
148		}
149	}
150	
151	// TestUpZeroHostnameNoResolverNoLoop is acceptance (4): a config with ZERO hostname specs (an
152	// all-literal edge peer) constructs NO resolver and starts NO re-resolution loop — asserted via
153	// the wiring seam (the resolver factory is never invoked). The literal endpoint is still installed
154	// on the engine peer at boot, so a single-IP-literal deployment is byte-for-byte the pre-G5 render.
155	func TestUpZeroHostnameNoResolverNoLoop(t *testing.T) {
156		cfg := writeEdgeConfig(t, `["127.0.0.1:51821"]`, false)
157		chtun := tuntest.NewChannelTUN()
158	
159		factoryCalls := 0
160		factory := func() (dnsresolve.Resolver, error) {
161			factoryCalls++
162			return &dnsresolve.FakeResolver{Hosts: map[string][]netip.Addr{}}, nil
163		}
164	
165		tun, err := up(cfg, discardLogger(t), chtun.TUN(), "wanbondtest0", factory)
166		if err != nil {
167			t.Fatalf("up on an all-literal edge peer failed: %v", err)
168		}
169		defer tun.Close()
170	
171		if factoryCalls != 0 {
172			t.Fatalf("resolver factory invoked %d times for a zero-hostname config, want 0 (provably inert, Q29)", factoryCalls)
173		}
174	
175		uapi, err := tun.dev.IpcGet()
176		if err != nil {
177			t.Fatalf("IpcGet: %v", err)
178		}
179		if !strings.Contains(uapi, "endpoint=127.0.0.1:51821") {
180			t.Fatalf("literal endpoint not installed at boot, want endpoint=127.0.0.1:51821:\n%s", uapi)
181		}
182	}
183	
184	// TestCloseStopsResolutionLoopNoLeak is acceptance (3): Close stops the re-resolution loop (and the
185	// probe/reconcile/hub-failover loops) with no goroutine leak. goleak verifies zero surviving
186	// goroutines AFTER Close, under -race.
187	func TestCloseStopsResolutionLoopNoLeak(t *testing.T) {
188		defer goleak.VerifyNone(t) // deferred first ⇒ runs LAST, after tun.Close below
189	
190		cfg := writeEdgeConfig(t, `["hub.example.com:51820"]`, true)
191		chtun := tuntest.NewChannelTUN()
192	
193		factory := func() (dnsresolve.Resolver, error) {
194			return &dnsresolve.FakeResolver{Hosts: map[string][]netip.Addr{}}, nil
195		}
196	
197		tun, err := up(cfg, discardLogger(t), chtun.TUN(), "wanbondtest0", factory)
198		if err != nil {
199			t.Fatalf("up: %v", err)
200		}
201		// Let the loops run a few probe cadences so their goroutines are genuinely live before Close.
202		time.Sleep(3 * telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval)
203		tun.Close()
204		// A second Close is idempotent and must not double-stop or panic.
205		tun.Close()
206	}
207	
208	// TestFirstResolveInstallsEndpointAndInitiatesHandshake is acceptance (2), the R70 FIRST-RESOLVE
209	// INSTALL PATH end to end against a REAL engine + real multipath bind (channel TUN, loopback
210	// socket). A hostname-only peer boots endpoint-less; the first successful resolve must (a) INSTALL
211	// the resolved endpoint on the engine peer via the UAPI/IpcSet path — so the engine peer reports it
212	// through UAPI get — AND (b) rehandshake, so a real handshake initiation actually egresses toward
213	// the resolved address (observed at a loopback listener). A fake-rehandshake-counter increment
214	// alone is NOT sufficient (R70): SetPeerRemote never populates the engine peer endpoint, so without
215	// the install the initiation would have no endpoint to transmit to.
216	func TestFirstResolveInstallsEndpointAndInitiatesHandshake(t *testing.T) {
217		lg := discardLogger(t)
218	
219		// A loopback UDP listener stands in for the resolved concentrator address.
220		conc, err := net.ListenUDP("udp", &net.UDPAddr{IP: net.IPv4(127, 0, 0, 1)})
221		if err != nil {
222			t.Fatalf("listen concentrator socket: %v", err)
223		}
224		defer conc.Close()
225		concAP := netip.MustParseAddrPort(conc.LocalAddr().String())
226	
227		// Real multipath bind over one loopback path with an AlwaysUp scheduler, so the scheduler never
228		// gates the initiation (a real prober would boot DOWN with no responder and Send would drop it).
229		psk := keyFromRaw(t, mustRandom(t, 32))
230		paths := []config.Path{{Name: "a", SourceAddr: netip.MustParseAddr("127.0.0.1")}}
231		scheduler, err := sched.NewActiveBackup([]sched.PathHealth{sched.AlwaysUp{}}, sched.Config{FailbackAfter: time.Second}, telemetry.SystemClock{}, lg)
232		if err != nil {
233			t.Fatalf("build scheduler: %v", err)
234		}
235		mp, err := bind.NewMultipath(paths, psk, scheduler, nil, nil, nil, nil, config.Amnezia{})
236		if err != nil {
237			t.Fatalf("build multipath bind: %v", err)
238		}
239	
240		// Real engine over a channel TUN, brought up endpoint-less (no endpoint= line).
241		chtun := tuntest.NewChannelTUN()
242		dev := awgdevice.NewDevice(chtun.TUN(), mp, engineLogger(lg, "error"))
243		defer dev.Close()
244	
245		edgePrivRaw, _ := genX25519(t)
246		_, hubPubRaw := genX25519(t)
247		var uapi strings.Builder
248		fmt.Fprintf(&uapi, "private_key=%s\n", hex.EncodeToString(edgePrivRaw))
249		fmt.Fprintf(&uapi, "public_key=%s\n", hex.EncodeToString(hubPubRaw))
250		fmt.Fprintf(&uapi, "allowed_ip=0.0.0.0/0\n")
251		if err := dev.IpcSet(uapi.String()); err != nil {
252			t.Fatalf("IpcSet endpoint-less crypto config: %v", err)
253		}
254		if err := dev.Up(); err != nil {
255			t.Fatalf("dev.Up: %v", err)
256		}
257	
258		// The controller as the device wires it: install to the engine peer's UAPI endpoint path,
259		// rehandshake to the engine peer, repoint to the bind. A single hostname spec, EMPTY at boot.
260		hubKey := keyFromRaw(t, hubPubRaw)
261		specs := []failoverSpec{nameSpec("hub.example.com", 51820)}
262		hp := []hubHealth{&fakeHealth{telemetry.StateUp}}
263		clk := &fakeClock{now: time.Unix(1000, 0)}
264		ctrl := newHubFailoverFromSpecs(specs, hp, mp, deviceRehandshake(dev, hubKey), clk, testSettle, lg)
265		ctrl.install = deviceInstallEndpoint(dev, hubKey, lg)
266	
267		// Before any resolution the engine peer has NO endpoint.
268		if before, err := dev.IpcGet(); err != nil {
269			t.Fatalf("IpcGet (pre-resolve): %v", err)
270		} else if strings.Contains(before, "endpoint=") {
271			t.Fatalf("engine peer had an endpoint before the first resolve, want none:\n%s", before)
272		}
273	
274		// FIRST successful resolve: boot adoption must install on the engine peer and rehandshake.
275		ctrl.updateResolution(0, []netip.AddrPort{concAP})
276	
277		// (a) The engine peer now reports the resolved endpoint via UAPI get.
278		after, err := dev.IpcGet()
279		if err != nil {
280			t.Fatalf("IpcGet (post-resolve): %v", err)
281		}
282		if !strings.Contains(after, "endpoint="+concAP.String()) {
283			t.Fatalf("engine peer endpoint not installed on first resolve, want endpoint=%s:\n%s", concAP, after)
284		}

content.content:
feat(device): boot-time DNS resolution lifecycle + R70 first-resolve install path (T74)

Wire the edge DNS re-resolution controller (T73) into the device lifecycle:

- Bounded initial resolve at Up: resolve each hostname endpoint spec under the
  [dns] per-lookup timeout and build the engine/UAPI peer endpoint ONLY from
  resolved entries. A name that does not resolve in the boot window brings the
  tunnel up WITHOUT a peer endpoint (tolerant boot, Q30) — the concentrator runs
  endpoint-less, so the engine supports it.
- FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH (R70): SetPeerRemote repoints the bind's per-path
  remotes but NEVER populates the engine peer's endpoint, which is set only by a
  UAPI `endpoint=` line through Multipath.ParseEndpoint. Boot adoption of an
  endpoint-less peer now INSTALLS the resolved endpoint on the engine peer via
  deviceInstallEndpoint (IpcSet endpoint=) THEN rehandshakes, so the initiation
  has an addressable endpoint. Subsequent re-resolves take the SetPeerRemote
  repoint path (engine virtual endpoint stays stable per A1).
- Resolver constructed ONCE, and only when some peer carries a hostname spec —
  a zero-hostname config builds no resolver and starts no loop (Q29 inertness).
- Separate stopResolution stopper on Tunnel, invoked by Close between
  stopHubFailover and dev.Close.

Up now delegates to an injectable up() seam (TUN + resolver factory) so device
tests drive a real engine + multipath bind over a channel TUN and a fake
resolver without privileged tun.CreateTUN.

Tests (go test ./internal/device/... -race):
- tolerant endpoint-less boot on a never-resolving single-hostname peer
- first resolve installs the engine peer endpoint (UAPI get) AND egresses a real
  handshake initiation toward the resolved address (observed at a loopback bind)
- Close stops the loop with no goroutine leak (goleak)
- zero-hostname config constructs no resolver / starts no loop (factory seam)
Controller boot-adoption tests updated to assert the install path (not
SetPeerRemote); verified non-vacuous by reverting the install to SetPeerRemote.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

610	// (index 0 = the preferred primary). The returned probers ARE the scheduler's
611	// PathHealth sources (a *Prober is internally synchronized, satisfying the
612	// PathHealth concurrency contract — a bare *Liveness would not) and are handed to
613	// the bind so the probe transport drives the very same liveness the scheduler
614	// selects on. This replaces the T15 sched.AlwaysUp placeholder with real on-wire
615	// failover (T37).
616	func buildScheduler(cfg *config.Config, lg log.Logger) (sched.Scheduler, []*telemetry.Prober, bind.ProberFactory, error) {
617		clock := telemetry.SystemClock{}
618		proberCfg := telemetry.ProberConfig{
619			LossWindow: telemetry.DefaultLossWindow,
620			Liveness: telemetry.LivenessConfig{
621				DownAfter:        telemetry.DefaultDownAfter,
622				UpAfterSuccesses: telemetry.DefaultUpSuccesses,
623			},
624		}
625		// One random per-boot session id shared by every path's Prober (it identifies
626		// this boot, not the path): a peer restart presents a new session id that resets
627		// the surviving responder's anti-replay high-water so liveness recovers (T38, D12).
628		// A runtime-added path (T30) reuses the SAME session id, so its probes join this
629		// boot's stream and the peer's reflector adopts them without a challenge reset.
630		sessionID, err := telemetry.NewSessionID(rand.Reader)
631		if err != nil {
632			return nil, nil, nil, err
633		}
634		// newProber mints one path's Prober with the shared session/config/clock. It is
635		// the single construction point for boot-time AND runtime paths, so both measure
636		// liveness identically.
637		newProber := func(name string, id uint8) *telemetry.Prober {
638			return telemetry.NewProber(name, id, sessionID, cfg.PSK, proberCfg, clock, lg)
639		}
640		probers := make([]*telemetry.Prober, len(cfg.Paths))
641		health := make([]sched.PathHealth, len(cfg.Paths))
642		quality := make([]sched.PathQuality, len(cfg.Paths))
643		for i := range cfg.Paths {
644			probers[i] = newProber(cfg.Paths[i].Name, uint8(i))
645			health[i] = probers[i]
646			quality[i] = probers[i]
647		}
648		// Policy is a config choice: active-backup (P1, default) or the T21 weighted-
649		// aggregation policy. Both consume the SAME per-path *Prober set — a *Prober
650		// satisfies BOTH PathHealth (liveness) and PathQuality (RTT/loss Estimate) — so the
651		// probe transport drives the very liveness/quality the scheduler selects on, and the
652		// swap is behind config with no Bind change.
653		scheduler, err := selectScheduler(cfg, health, quality, clock, lg)
654		if err != nil {
655			return nil, nil, nil, err
656		}
657		return scheduler, probers, newProber, nil
658	}
659	
660	// selectScheduler builds the send scheduler the configured policy names, over the
661	// per-path health (and, for the weighted policy, quality) sources. active-backup is
662	// the P1 default; weighted is T21. The weighted knobs are validated at config load,
663	// so translating them here cannot fail on range — only NewWeighted's structural
664	// checks (which the wiring satisfies) apply.

input.description: Find test coverage of production wiring and probe interval value

content.text:
All evidence gathered. Consolidating the review.

**Verification performed**

1. **Gate**: `go test ./internal/device/... -race -count=1` — pass; full `go vet ./... && go build ./... && gofmt && go test ./... -count=1` via `nix develop` — all packages pass.
2. **Tolerant boot (acceptance 1)**: `TestUpTolerantBootEndpointless` drives the real `up()` seam with a never-resolving resolver on a single-hostname peer; asserts `Up` succeeds, factory called exactly once, lookup attempted, and `IpcGet` contains no `endpoint=`. Operationally satisfied.
3. **ParseEndpoint stays IP-only**: `multipath.go` untouched; the engine only ever receives `netip.AddrPort.String()` (from `bootEndpoint.ap` in `uapiConfig` and from `deviceInstallEndpoint`). No hostname can reach the bind.
4. **R70 egress evidence (acceptance 2)**: `TestFirstResolveInstallsEndpointAndInitiatesHandshake` uses a real engine + real multipath bind over loopback, boots endpoint-less, and after `updateResolution` asserts BOTH `endpoint=<resolved>` in `IpcGet` AND an actual UDP datagram received at the resolved listener — not a counter. The only possible egress source is the initiation (no probers, no keepalive). Assertion quality is genuine.
5. **Close ordering / leaks (acceptance 3)**: `stopResolution` invoked in `Close` between `stopHubFailover` and `dev.Close`; goleak verifies zero survivors under `-race`, double-`Close` exercised.
6. **Q29 inertness (acceptance 4)**: zero-hostname config → factory never invoked, literal endpoint still rendered; asserted via the seam.
7. **Reload untouched — legitimate scope call**: `Reload` is path-membership-only by design (T30); a `wireguard` section change (where endpoint specs live) is never applied — `reloadWarnings` explicitly warns "wireguard section changed". Since spec changes are never applied, there is no applied-state divergence for the loop to chase; restarting the loop on an ignored change would be wrong. No gap.
8. **Concentrator safety**: config validation rejects concentrator peers with `EndpointSpecs` (config.go:1008) and `dns` opt-in (config.go:1015), so the boot-resolve/resolver path is structurally edge-only.

**The one finding**: the production wiring of the R70 install path has zero test coverage. `grep` confirms no test calls `startFailoverAndResolution`; the end-to-end test hand-wires `ctrl.install = deviceInstallEndpoint(...)` itself, duplicating rather than driving the load-bearing line in `failover.go` (`ctrl.install = deviceInstallEndpoint(dev, peer.PublicKey, hlg)`) and the `boot.resolver`/`boot.specs` handoff through `startResolution`. Combined with `installActive`'s deliberate fallback (`install == nil` → `SetPeerRemote`), deleting or losing that one wiring line silently resurrects the exact R70 defect — endpoint-less peers never get an engine endpoint — while the entire suite stays green. A cheap `up()`-driven test closes this: fake resolver that fails the boot lookup then succeeds; the resolution loop's first step (armed at construction, stepped at `DefaultProbeInterval` = 200ms) boot-adopts unconditionally (not liveness-gated), so `IpcGet` shows the installed endpoint within ~1s — no probe responder needed (the egress half is already proven at the hand-wired tier, where `AlwaysUp` was correctly used since real probers boot DOWN and would drop the initiation).

### Session summary
- **Did:** reviewed task T74 round 1 (diff 352c1e5..a66df75 in /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-afa803909d4c484b6); re-ran the full gate incl. `-race` via nix develop
- **Achieved:** verdict disapprove; 1 criticism / 0 questions
- **Discovered:** acceptance items 1, 3, 4 verified end-to-end through `up()`; item 2's install+egress evidence is genuine (real engine+bind, real datagram observed) but bypasses the production wiring — `startFailoverAndResolution`'s `ctrl.install` assignment and the `boot.resolver` handoff execute in no test, and the `installActive` nil-fallback makes that omission regress silently to the R70 defect
- **Issues:** none
